metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_5353
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Independently of its cholesterol transport, overexpression of hORP2 (human gene OSBPL2) in wildtype neurons is deleterious for vesicle priming.

"The reduced priming but unaltered docking in hORP2-overexpressing neurons could be due to changes in the localization or conformation of the SNARE complexes and/or their components, as ORP2 interacts with Stx1A and SNAP-25 (Figure 1).21 Initially, we performed Stx1A-Syb2 co-immunoprecipitations in hOPR2-overexpressing neurons to reveal possible alterations in SNARE complex assembly. However, the interactions between Stx1A and Syb2 were only slightly reduced in hORP2-overexpressing neurons, suggesting no general disturbance in ternary SNARE complex levels (Figure 7E).

Using heterologous HEK293T cells for co-immunoprecipitation assays, we identified the Stx1A N-peptide (Stx1A amino acids 2-28) as the hORP2 interaction site (Figure 7F). Simultaneous expression of a hORP2 binding-deficient Stx1A(ΔN) in hORP2-overexpressing neurons reverted the priming defect caused by hORP2 overexpression (hORP2 + Stx1A) to 84% of control (vector + Stx1A) levels (v + Stx1A: 1.00 ± 0.14, hORP2 + Stx1A: 0.51 ± 0.07, hORP2 + Stx1A(ΔN): 0.84 ± 0.10; Figure 7G). This result suggests that the hORP2-mediated priming defect requires an interaction of hORP2 and the Stx1A N-peptide. Similarly to hORP2, the SM protein Munc18-1 was shown to interact with the Stx1A N-peptide.

Employing HEK293T cells, we performed Stx1A immunoprecipitations with hORP2, Munc18-1, or hORP2/Munc18-1 co-expression. These experiments revealed competitive binding of hORP2 and Munc18-1 to Stx1A, suggesting a common interaction surface within the Stx1A N-peptide (Figure 7H). Correspondingly, Munc18-1 co-immunoprecipitation with Stx1A was profoundly reduced in hORP2 overexpressing neurons (Figure 7I). In summary, these results suggest an ORP2-mediated priming defect due to masking of the Stx1A N-peptide binding site, leading to a reduced interaction of Stx1A with Munc18-1.". Experimental description: mice were used for all experiments, but the overexpressed hORP2 protein in Figure 7 was human;
"Human ORP2 (NM144498.3) was cloned in frame after the P2A site into BL-140 to generate BL-1210 (f(syn)NLS.GFP-P2A-hOPR2-w)."

NCBI accession NM_144498 referred to here matches uniprot accession Q9H1P3
